vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'detergent'. After using Tide detergent, clothes start shining and radiating in light. So, refulgent means bright and shining.
Sounds like 'at depth'. When you know your subject at depth, you are adept. So, adept means an expert at something.
Sounds like 'Ali + genie'. Genie always said 'Jo hukum mere aaka' to Aladdin. He remained loyal to him till the end. Hence, allegiance means loyalty.
Has 'Banta' in it. Banta is a famous character of Santa Banta joke series. Banta always keeps on joking. So, bantering means joking talk.
Sounds like 'internal inspection'. When a person looks inside himself to know more about himself, then it is called introspection.
Sounds like 'men + able'. A mother is advising her son to become an able man who considers everyone's opinion. So, amenable means docile and obedient.
Sounds like 'genuine'. Genuine people are straightforward and innocent, just like children. So, ingenuous means artless and innocent.
Sounds like 'fest'. At an inter-college fest, everyone participates with great energy. Hence, zest means enthusiasm and zeal.
Sounds like 'up ho gi'. When you climb up and up, you will reach the highest point. So, apogee means the highest stage.
Sounds like 'be + little'. If you are criticizing someone, you are making him appear little in front of you. Hence, belittle means to criticize.
Sounds like 'a base'. To bring someone to the base level will be to humiliate him. So, abase means to humiliate.
Sounds like 'prawn'. Sometimes, wrestlers eat more prawn to become muscular and strong (brawn). Hence, brawn means strength.
Sounds like 'bad design'. A woman in the neighbourhood used to dress up very loudly with all bad designer clothes. Hence, bedizen means dress up gaudily.
Sounds like 'belly + cause'. Imagine there is nothing in your belly, you are hungry and because of this you are angry. Hence, bellicose means angry.
Sounds like a Hindi phrase 'in karagaar' (means jail). So, the word incarcerate means to imprison.
Sounds like 'bald in this age'. Suppose your friend is having hair fall and you are teasing him about being bald in this age. So, badinage means teasing playfully.
Sounds like 'enemy + us'. An enemy has nothing but hostility towards us. So, animus means to have animosity for someone.
Sounds like 'alter your nation'. The NRI got into an altercation with the authorities because they asked him to alter his nation. So, altercation means dispute.
Sounds like 'the + fire'. Winds blow out the fire. Hence, zephyr is the wind that blows out the fire.

vocab.meaning: Somewhat acid or harsh in taste or manner
vocab.memoryKey: Derived from the word acid, which is sharp and harsh. So, acidulous means somewhat acid or harsh in taste or manner.

vocab.meaning: To attack by surprise from a hidden place
vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'I am in the bush'. A person is hiding in the bush, so he can attack his enemy by surprise. So, ambush means to attack by surprise.
Sounds like 'a nail'. To toughen a nail, annealing is done. Hence, anneal means to toughen.
Sounds like 'riddle'. A riddle is confusing. So, addle means confused and puzzled.
Sounds like 'add ornaments'. It is done to decorate something. So, adorn means to decorate and to garnish something.
Can be memorized using cartoon character 'Goofy'. Goofy was famous for committing mistakes and blunders. So, something that Goofy does is gaffe.
Sounds like 'annual + duty'. As a duty, I pay her annual allowance for her work. So, annuity means specific income given at a specific time.
Sounds like 'confuse'. Something which confuses you is abstruse because it is hard to understand.
Sounds like Hindi 'bawaal'. Imagine there is a quarrel in the neighbourhood. Bawaal macha hua hai. So, brawl means noisy quarrel.
Sounds like Hindi 'jannat' (heaven), which is the highest point. Hence, zenith means summit or the highest point.
Sounds like 'flirt'. Imagine you are flirting with a pretty girl. You are so excited that you blurt out all the secrets of your friend.
Sounds like 'be nice son'. Imagine yourself touching your father's feet and receiving blessings of always being nice. Hence, benison means blessings.
Sounds like 'dear + left'. Imagine someone very dear to you has left the world. So, you are very sad. So, bereft means sad.

vocab.meaning: Heavy objects used to add stability and balance
vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'blast'. A ballast saves from blast by adding stability. Hence, ballast means something used to give stability.
Has 'grand' in it, which means something great and large. Hence, aggrandize means to enlarge or increase in size or intensity.
Sounds like 'belly + gents'. Imagine sumo wrestlers, gents with huge bellies, always angry and ready to fight. So, belligerent means quarrelsome.
Sounds like 'man in the city'. Relatives warn a girl that men in the city are liars, be careful. So, mendacity means lie or deceit.
Sounds like 'blah blah blah'. When we talk about unimportant things, people will listen it as blah blah blah. So, babble means to chatter foolishly.
Sounds like 'benefit + act'. A person who acts for the benefit of others is a benefactor.
Sounds like 'add more duration'. I owe him money but I requested him to give me some more duration. So, adjuration means earnest plea or appeal.

vocab.meaning: To wave something in the air in a threatening or excited way
vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'brandy'. After drinking brandy, we become drunk and we wave. Hence, brandishing means waving.
Sounds like 'garnish'. The dessert was looking ordinary, but after garnishing, it turned garish. So, garish means showy and gaudy.
Sounds like 'painful'. If evil acts are done by bad people, it is painful for good people. Hence, baleful means evil.
Opposite of 'firmity'. To remain firm means to be strong. So, infirmity means physical weakness.
Sounds like 'AAP + all'. Imagine AAP lost all its seats. People will be shocked. So, appall means disgusted and shocked.
Sounds like 'be + nice'. Your mother tells you to be nice and friendly. Hence, benign means being nice.
Sounds like 'Johnny'. Johnny Lever is a famous comedian in Bollywood. Hence, being comical like Johnny is zany.
Derived from 'benefit + dictation'. Saying something to cause benefit is a blessing. So, benediction means blessings.
Relate to cricketer Praveen Kumar. He became rich after joining the Indian cricket team. So, parvenu refers to someone who has recently acquired wealth.
Sounds like 'addition'. On addition, accretion occurs. Hence, accretion means growth or accumulation.
Hindi mnemonic 'Moti + fly'. A fat girl fled from a party because she felt ashamed of her massive body. So, mortify means humiliation.
Sounds like 'kaun phone'. Imagine 50 people shouting 'kaun hai phone pe?!' at once. Pure chaos and noise. So, cacophony means horrible, jarring sound.
Sounds like 'the bill it ate'. The restaurant bill was so huge, it ate up all your energy and money. You're left weak and drained. Debilitate = to weaken completely.
Sounds like 'a brilliant'. A student who scores 99.9 percentile in CAT becomes bubbling with joy — jumping, shouting, celebrating. That's ebullient — overflowing with excitement!
Sounds like 'fast + hideous'. Your mom says 'jaldi karo!' but you say 'fast kaam hideous hota hai!' You want everything perfect and neat. Fastidious = extremely fussy.
Sounds like 'girl + us'. Bhai jab girls humare saath hoti hain, sab non-stop bakte hain! Everyone becomes excessively talkative. Garrulous = talks way too much.
Think 'happiness-less'. No happiness = no luck. Like a guy who studies 6 months for CAT but sleeps through the exam alarm. Totally hapless — unlucky and pitiful.
Sounds like 'I'm a pet lover'. You see a puppy on the road and impulsively take it home without thinking. Your mom is furious! Impetuous = acting without thinking.
Sounds like 'just + oppose'. When you put two opposite things next to each other — like a topper's answer sheet next to yours — that painful comparison is juxtaposing.
Sounds like 'cow + tow'. Try towing a cow — even the cow bows its head in defeat! Kowtow = to bow down submissively, like you do before your strict professor.
Sounds like 'lunch + kid'. After a heavy lunch, every kid becomes lazy and sleepy. That post-biryani drowsiness is languid — completely lacking energy.
Think of the movie Top Gun — Maverick is the pilot who breaks every rule and does things his own way. A maverick doesn't follow the crowd.
Sounds like 'never + fair + us'. A nefarious person is never fair to anyone. They're always scheming evil plans. Nefarious = extremely wicked.
Sounds like 'O bhai, obvious!' But the person still doesn't get it — they're completely unaware of what's happening around them. Oblivious = totally clueless.
Sounds like 'pen + chant'. Imagine a poet who chants while holding a pen — he has a strong liking for writing poetry. Penchant = a strong inclination towards something.
Sounds like 'kwag + mire (mud)'. Imagine stepping into deep mud — the more you struggle, the deeper you sink. A quagmire is a messy situation you can't escape easily.
Sounds like 'rancid + our'. Our rancid, spoiled friendship left nothing but bitterness. Rancour = deep-seated hatred and ill-feeling.
Sounds like 'sage + aashish'. A sage gives you aashirvaad (blessings) with wise words. Sagacious = having sharp judgment and wisdom.
Sounds like 'ta + sit + turn'. A taciturn person just sits and turns away — never speaks. Like that one friend in the group who says 5 words the entire evening.
Sounds like 'you be quit us'. No matter where you go to quit us, we're everywhere! Like Jio network — ubiquitous, present all over India.
Sounds like 'was + late'. He vacillated so much between options that he was late for everything! Vacillate = to keep going back and forth, unable to decide.
Sounds like 'woh + aunty'. Woh aunty spends recklessly — shopping without any self-control! Wanton = deliberately cruel or reckless without care.
Xeno = foreign, phobia = fear. Simple! Imagine someone scared of eating Chinese food because it's foreign. Xenophobia = irrational fear of anything foreign.

vocab.meaning: A loyal, hardworking servant or helper
vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'yeah + man'. Your most reliable friend who always says 'yeah man, I'm on it!' and does all the hard work loyally. Yeoman = faithful, diligent helper.
Sounds like 'zeal + lot'. Someone with way too much zeal — a lot of it. They take their passion to an extreme, fanatical level. Zealot = an overly enthusiastic fanatic.
Sounds like 'absent + scond (second)'. In one second, the thief became absent — he absconded! Ran away secretly before the police came.
Sounds like 'be + girl'. Boys try to 'be' impressive in front of a girl — they charm and enchant. But sometimes it's all deception! Beguile = to charm or trick someone.
Sounds like 'can + do + r'. 'I can do it, and I'll tell you honestly!' That's candour — speaking with complete frankness and honesty.
Sounds like 'earth'. Imagine the earth has run out of water — a severe shortage! Dearth = an extreme scarcity of something.
Sounds like 'elephant'. An elephant walks with grace and command — similarly, an eloquent speaker speaks with grace and commands attention. Eloquent = beautifully spoken.
Sounds like 'fruit + thief'. Imagine a kid stealing mangoes furtively — looking left and right, sneaking quietly. Furtive = doing something secretively to avoid being noticed.
Sounds like 'group + areas'. A gregarious person is always found in group areas — canteen, party, chai stall. They love being around people. Gregarious = super social.
Sounds like 'he + done + it'. He's done it all — partying, travelling, eating out. He lives only for pleasure. A hedonist = someone whose life goal is enjoyment.

vocab.meaning: Rebel against traditions, critic of beliefs
vocab.memoryKey: Icon + clash. Someone who clashes with icons and traditions. Think of someone who questions every festival, every ritual — 'why do we do this?' Iconoclast = tradition-breaker.
Sounds like 'joy + wala'. The 'joy-wala' person — always laughing, always happy, the life of every party. Jovial = full of happiness and high spirits.
Sounds like 'knock'. Some people have the knack of knocking on the right doors — natural talent to find opportunities. Knack = a special skill that comes naturally.
Sounds like 'low + quash'. You can't quash (stop) a loquacious person even at low volume — they'll keep talking no matter what! Loquacious = can't stop talking.
Magna = great, animus = spirit. Great spirit! A magnanimous person forgives even their enemies. Like forgiving your friend who ate your tiffin. Magnanimous = big-hearted.
Named after Narcissus from Greek mythology — a man so obsessed with his own reflection that he fell into a lake staring at himself. Narcissist = obsessively in love with oneself.
Sounds like 'O + Pullman'. 'O wow, Pullman hotel!' — extremely luxurious and rich interiors. Opulent = displaying great wealth and luxury.
Sounds like 'perfect + hideous'. Looks perfect from outside, but hideous (treacherous) from inside. Like a backstabbing friend who smiles at you but plots against you.
Sounds like 'quarrel + us'. Someone who quarrels with everyone by constantly complaining. 'Yeh nahi chahiye, woh nahi chahiye.' Querulous = always cribbing.
Sounds like 'rapa + cious (seize)'. Imagine someone rapidly seizing everything — money, food, property. Extremely greedy! Rapacious = aggressively greedy.
Sounds like 'psycho + aunt'. Your psycho aunt who flatters everyone to get what she wants — 'aap toh bahut sundar ho, beta toh genius hai!' Sycophant = shameless flatterer.
Sounds like 'trip + addition'. You're going on a trip but the addition to the plan scares you — bungee jumping! That nervous fear is trepidation.
Sounds like 'uncle + thus'. That uncle at weddings who's overly sweet — 'beta, tum toh bahut bade ho gaye!' Dripping with fake praise. Unctuous = oily and insincere.
Sounds like 'win + addictive'. Winning is so addictive for this person that they'll take revenge on anyone who defeats them. Vindictive = driven by revenge.
Sounds like 'whim + sical (cycle)'. Riding a cycle based on whims — suddenly turning left, then right, then doing a wheelie. Totally unpredictable! Whimsical = acting on sudden fancies.
Sounds like 'eggs + irritate'. Imagine you're already sick and someone keeps throwing eggs at you — making it worse! Exacerbate = to make a bad situation even worse.
Sounds like 'free + walrus'. Imagine a free walrus roaming in your college — silly and ridiculous! Nobody takes it seriously. Frivolous = lacking any seriousness.
Sounds like 'gul + lable'. If you 'gul' (swallow in Hindi) every label without checking, you're gullible. You believe anything anyone tells you. Gullible = easily fooled.
Sounds like 'her + anger'. When her anger explodes, she delivers a long, aggressive lecture that goes on and on. That unstoppable angry speech is a harangue.
Im + mutable (mute = change). 'I'm not mutable!' — I cannot be changed. Like your mother's rule — 'jab tak padhai nahi, TV nahi.' That rule is immutable.
Think of the quiz show 'Jeopardy'. In the show, you put your money at risk with every answer. Jeopardize = to put something valuable in danger.
Amazon Kindle lights up knowledge just like kindling lights up fire. Kindle = to spark something — whether it's fire or inspiration.
Sounds like 'let + her + jic (sleep)'. Let her sleep — she's so lethargic after the heavy rajma chawal lunch. Lethargic = zero energy, total laziness.
Sounds like 'medical + us'. Doctors (medical people) have to be extremely careful and precise — one wrong cut and it's over! Meticulous = paying great attention to detail.
Sounds like 'none + shall + rant'. Nothing makes this person rant — they stay cool and unbothered about everything. 'CAT result? Chill hai bhai.' Nonchalant = effortlessly calm.
Sounds like 'ostrich + size'. An ostrich is so large that the other birds kick it out of the group — excluded! Ostracize = to deliberately exclude someone from a group.
Sounds like 'please + kate'. Kate is angry and you're begging 'please Kate, calm down!' Placate = to make someone less angry or upset.
Sounds like 'kaunsa dairy?' You're standing between Amul and Mother Dairy — which one to buy? That confusion is a quandary — a state of not knowing what to do.
Sounds like 'retire + scent'. A retired person sitting silently, smelling flowers. Quiet and reserved. Reticent = not willing to speak freely.
Sounds like 'Spurs' (Tottenham). Tottenham Spurs fans claim they'll win the league — that's completely spurious and fake every season! Spurious = not genuine.
Sounds like 'ten + nails'. Imagine holding on with ten nails dug deep — you won't let go no matter what. Tenacious = holding firmly, never giving up.

vocab.meaning: Seize power illegally, take by force
vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'use + roop (form)'. A villain uses someone's roop (disguise) to seize the throne illegally. Usurp = to grab power that isn't yours.
Verb + dose. A huge dose of verbs (words)! When someone uses 500 words to say what could be said in 5. Verbose = using way more words than needed.
Sounds like 'wish + full'. Full of wishes for the past — remembering college days, old friends, childhood summers. That bittersweet longing is wistful.
Sounds like 'ex + honour + ate'. They ate his honour (blamed him) but later he was declared innocent — his ex-honour was restored! Exonerate = to declare someone not guilty.
Sounds like 'flip + ant'. Imagine flipping an ant off the table casually — showing zero respect for it. Flippant = not taking things seriously, being casually rude.
Sounds like 'gratuity + us'. They charged us gratuity even though the service was terrible — totally unnecessary and uncalled for! Gratuitous = without good reason.

vocab.meaning: Forerunner, herald, sign of what's coming
vocab.memoryKey: Sounds like 'harbour + danger'. Dark clouds over the harbour are a harbinger of the coming storm. Harbinger = a sign that something is about to happen.
Sounds like 'in + sip + id'. You take one sip and your id (soul) dies — it's that tasteless! Hostel food without salt. Insipid = lacking flavour or interest.
Sounds like 'judge + us'. A judge has to be wise and sensible when judging us. Judicious = showing good sense and careful thinking.
Think of the movie Labyrinth — a giant confusing maze you can't escape. Old Delhi galiyan (lanes) are a labyrinth — once you enter, good luck finding your way out!
Sounds like 'Monday'. Monday is the most mundane, boring day of the week — back to routine, nothing exciting. Mundane = dull and ordinary.
Sounds like 'nosta + aligah (Aligarh)'. Missing your hometown Aligarh while studying in a different city. That homesick feeling is nostalgia — yearning for old times.
Sounds like 'Oh my + nas'. 'Oh my god, meri naas (destruction) aa rahi hai!' Dark clouds, eerie silence — something bad is coming. Ominous = giving signs of bad things ahead.
Sounds like 'purse + money + us'. He keeps his purse and money from us — never treats anyone! That kanjoos uncle is parsimonious — extremely stingy with money.
Quint = five, essential = important. The five most essential qualities combined = the perfect example. Shah Rukh Khan is the quintessential Bollywood hero — the classic ideal.
Sounds like 're + call + I + can't'. 'You can recall me but I can't come!' — a defiant, stubborn response. Recalcitrant = refusing to obey authority.
Sounds like 'so + poor + ific (terrific)'. The lecture was so poorly terrific that everyone fell asleep! Soporific = so boring it puts you to sleep.
Sounds like 'truck + lent'. Imagine a truck driver who won't lend his parking spot — he gets fierce and aggressive about it! Truculent = ready to fight at any moment.
Un + tenable (hold). Cannot be held or defended. Like arguing that samosa is healthier than salad — your position is untenable, it just can't stand!
Sounds like 'we + narrate'. 'We narrate stories about our grandparents with deep respect.' Venerate = to honour someone with great admiration, like touching elders' feet.
Think of the waning moon — it gets smaller and smaller every night until it almost disappears. Wane = to gradually decrease in power or size.
Sounds like 'a + female + role'. In old Bollywood, a female role was ephemeral — 2 songs and disappeared! Ephemeral = lasting for a very short time.
Sounds like 'fortunate + us'. A fortuitous event is one that's fortunate for us — happening by lucky chance. Like randomly guessing correctly in CAT!
Sounds like 'he + jee + money'. He has JEE degree AND money — naturally, he dominates everyone! Hegemony = dominance of one group over others.
Idiot + sin + crazy. An idiotic, sinful, crazy habit that only you have — like eating maggi with curd. That's your idiosyncrasy — a personal quirk nobody understands.
Named after Lord Jagannath's massive chariot in Puri — once it starts rolling, nothing can stop it! A juggernaut is any powerful, unstoppable force.
Sounds like 'clement + sea'. A clement (gentle) sea is calm and merciful to sailors. Clemency = showing mercy and forgiveness, like a judge reducing a sentence.
Sounds like 'Draco' from Harry Potter — Draco Malfoy was harsh and cruel. Draconian = excessively harsh rules or punishments. Like 'phone submit at hostel gate' rules!
Sounds like 'energy + wait'. All your energy says 'wait, I'm leaving!' You're drained completely. Enervate = to make someone feel drained and lifeless.
Sounds like 'fill + buster'. Someone who fills time like a buster — talking endlessly in Parliament to block a bill from passing. Filibuster = talking non-stop to delay decisions.
Sounds like 'Germany'. If you're discussing European football, Germany is germane (relevant) to the topic. Germane = closely related to the subject at hand.
Hypo = under, thetical = thesis. Under a thesis — not yet proven, just imaginary. 'Hypothetically, agar mujhe 100 percentile aa jaye...' — it's theoretical, not real.
Im + pervious (previous). Not affected by previous attacks! Like a student who fails 5 mocks but is impervious to discouragement — nothing penetrates their confidence.
Juris (law) + prudence (wisdom). Wisdom of law! Jurisprudence = the study and theory of law. Law students study this to become wise judges.
Mis (hate) + anthrope (humans). Hates humans! That grumpy uncle who shouts at every kid, hates every neighbour, and talks to nobody. He's a misanthrope.
Sounds like 'practical + magic'. Not magic — practical! A pragmatic person deals with things sensibly and realistically, not based on dreams. 'IIM milega ya nahi, backup rakh!'
Sounds like 'sangeen (serious in Hindi)'. But flip it! A sanguine person takes even serious situations with a positive smile. 'CAT exam kharab gaya? Koi nahi, next year!' Always optimistic.

idioms.meaning: To work or study late into the night
idioms.memoryKey: Picture a CAT aspirant at 3 AM — Red Bull finished, eyes burning, but still solving quant. The lamp oil is almost gone but the grind doesn't stop. Burning midnight oil = pulling an all-nighter.

idioms.meaning: Asking someone what they're thinking about
idioms.memoryKey: Your friend is zoning out during a CAT mock. You wave a coin and say 'ek paisa dunga, bata kya soch raha hai!' Penny for your thoughts = 'bhai, kya chal raha hai dimag mein?'

idioms.meaning: To avoid talking about the main topic
idioms.memoryKey: A hunter keeps hitting bushes around the rabbit but never aims at it. Like when your friend asks 'result kaisa tha?' and you start talking about the weather. Stop beating around the bush!

idioms.meaning: To endure a painful situation bravely
idioms.memoryKey: Old war soldiers bit actual bullets during surgery without anaesthesia. Like you biting the bullet and opening your CAT scorecard — painful but you gotta face it!

idioms.meaning: To initiate conversation in an awkward situation
idioms.memoryKey: First day at coaching class — everyone sitting like statues frozen in ice. Someone cracks a joke and CRACK — the ice breaks! Conversation flows. Break the ice = start talking in awkward silence.

idioms.phrase: Cost an arm and a leg
idioms.meaning: Something extremely expensive
idioms.memoryKey: IIM fees — 25 lakhs! Itna mehnga ki lagta hai haath-pair bech diye. When something costs an arm and a leg, it's insanely expensive.

idioms.meaning: To regret something that cannot be undone
idioms.memoryKey: You accidentally submitted the CAT exam 10 minutes early. Crying about it won't add those 10 minutes back. Don't cry over spilt milk — jo hua so hua!

idioms.phrase: Hit the nail on the head
idioms.meaning: To describe exactly what's causing a problem
idioms.memoryKey: A carpenter who drives the nail in one perfect strike — no wasted effort. Like when someone says 'tumhari problem hai ki tum time manage nahi karte' — boom, nail on the head!

idioms.phrase: Let the cat out of the bag
idioms.meaning: To reveal a secret accidentally
idioms.memoryKey: Surprise birthday party planned. But your dumb friend texts 'party ke liye kya laun?' in the group. The CAT (secret) is out of the bag! Accidentally exposed.

idioms.meaning: Something that happens very rarely
idioms.memoryKey: Moon turns blue almost never. Just like your kanjoos friend treating everyone — once in a blue moon! Extremely rare occurrence.

idioms.phrase: The ball is in your court
idioms.meaning: It's your turn to make a decision or take action
idioms.memoryKey: Badminton match — the shuttle is on your side. Your opponent has played their shot. Now it's YOUR turn to hit. Ball in your court = decision is yours now.

idioms.meaning: Looking in the wrong place or accusing the wrong person
idioms.memoryKey: A dog barking at a tree where the cat used to be — but the cat already climbed to another tree! The dog is wasting its time. Barking up the wrong tree = blaming the wrong person.

idioms.meaning: Destroying relationships or connections permanently
idioms.memoryKey: Imagine crossing a bridge and setting it on fire behind you — now you can never go back. Like fighting with your professor before results — you've burned that bridge forever!

idioms.meaning: Something that seems bad but turns out to be good
idioms.memoryKey: You didn't crack CAT, joined a startup instead, and became a crorepati. The failure was actually a blessing wearing a disguise — it looked bad but turned out amazing!

idioms.meaning: To do something in the cheapest or easiest way
idioms.memoryKey: Building a house but cutting corners on material to save money — fewer bricks, thinner walls. Cut corners = doing something lazily to save time or money. Never cut corners in CAT prep!

idioms.meaning: Someone who argues the opposite side for the sake of debate
idioms.memoryKey: In a GD (group discussion), one person deliberately argues the opposite view to test everyone's thinking. They're playing devil's advocate — not because they believe it, but to challenge you.

idioms.meaning: An obvious problem nobody wants to discuss
idioms.memoryKey: Imagine a literal elephant standing in your living room and everyone pretends it's not there. 'Nice weather today!' Elephant in the room = the big issue everyone's ignoring.

idioms.phrase: Get a taste of your own medicine
idioms.meaning: Experience the same bad treatment you give to others
idioms.memoryKey: The class bully gets bullied by the new kid. How does it feel now, bhai? Taste of your own medicine = suffering what you made others suffer.

idioms.meaning: To make more effort than is expected
idioms.memoryKey: Everyone stops after running 5 km. But you run 6 km — that extra mile. Like solving 100 extra questions when others stop at the syllabus. Going the extra mile = putting in extraordinary effort.

idioms.meaning: In serious trouble
idioms.memoryKey: Imagine being thrown into a bathtub of boiling hot water — AAAH! You're in serious trouble. Like getting caught using your phone during a CAT mock — you're in hot water now!

idioms.meaning: To follow a trend or join something popular
idioms.memoryKey: A band plays on a wagon and everyone jumps on to join the party. Like when one person starts a startup and suddenly everyone wants to be an 'entrepreneur'. Jumping on the bandwagon!

idioms.meaning: Stay positive during difficult times
idioms.memoryKey: When you're sad, your chin drops. But someone lifts it up — 'sar upar rakh bhai!' Keep your chin up = stay confident and hopeful even when things are tough.

idioms.phrase: Kill two birds with one stone
idioms.meaning: Achieve two things with a single action
idioms.memoryKey: One stone, two birds down. Like going to the library to study AND impress your crush — kill two birds with one stone. Maximum output, minimum effort!

idioms.meaning: Don't disturb a situation that's currently stable
idioms.memoryKey: A dog is sleeping peacefully. You poke it. It bites you. Your fault! Let sleeping dogs lie = don't bring up old fights or forgotten problems. Rehne de bhai!

idioms.meaning: To miss an opportunity by being too slow
idioms.memoryKey: The boat is leaving the harbour and you're still tying your shoelaces on the dock. Too late! You missed the boat. Like submitting your IIM application one day after the deadline.

idioms.meaning: To stop a problem before it grows bigger
idioms.memoryKey: A weed is just a tiny bud — easy to pluck now. But let it grow and it'll destroy the whole garden. Nip it in the bud = kill the problem while it's still small.

idioms.meaning: In a risky or dangerous situation
idioms.memoryKey: Walking on thin ice over a lake — one wrong step and CRACK, you fall in. Like bunking classes when you already have low attendance — you're on thin ice, buddy!

idioms.meaning: To joke or tease someone
idioms.memoryKey: Literally imagine pulling someone's leg — they stumble and everyone laughs. It's just a prank! Pull someone's leg = tease them harmlessly for fun.

idioms.meaning: To understand the hidden meaning
idioms.memoryKey: RC passages in CAT — the answer is never in the lines, it's BETWEEN them! You have to read what's implied, not stated. Read between the lines = understand the hidden message.

idioms.meaning: To reveal secret information
idioms.memoryKey: Imagine a jar full of secret beans. Someone tips it over and all the beans spill out — secrets exposed! Like your friend accidentally revealing the surprise party. Spilled the beans!

idioms.meaning: To take credit for someone else's achievement
idioms.memoryKey: You prepare an amazing presentation. But your teammate presents it first and gets all the applause — he stole your thunder! Taking credit for someone else's work.

idioms.meaning: Enjoying the advantages of two different things
idioms.memoryKey: Working from home — office salary + home ka khana. You get the best of both worlds! Enjoying two different advantages at the same time.

idioms.meaning: Through good and bad times together
idioms.memoryKey: Thick jungle and thin ice — your best friend walks with you through both. Through thick and thin = staying loyal in good times AND bad times.

idioms.meaning: Feeling slightly ill or unwell
idioms.memoryKey: Bad weather makes you feel gloomy and sick. Under the weather = 'tabiyat theek nahi hai yaar.' Not seriously ill, just feeling off.

idioms.meaning: Uncertain, not yet decided
idioms.memoryKey: Throw a coin in the air — while it's spinning, you don't know heads or tails. It's up in the air! Plans that aren't confirmed yet are up in the air.

idioms.meaning: Something that will never happen
idioms.memoryKey: Pigs flying? IMPOSSIBLE! 'I'll top CAT without studying — haan, when pigs fly!' Used sarcastically for things that'll never happen.

idioms.meaning: A big fuss about something unimportant
idioms.memoryKey: A tiny storm inside a tiny teacup — sounds dramatic but it's harmless. Like two friends fighting for 3 days over who ate the last biscuit. A storm in a teacup = overreacting!

idioms.meaning: What you do matters more than what you say
idioms.memoryKey: Your friend says 'I'll help you study' but never shows up. Meanwhile, another friend silently shares their notes daily. Actions > words. Always.

idioms.meaning: To make a bad situation worse
idioms.memoryKey: A small fire is burning. Instead of water, you throw petrol on it — BOOM! Like telling your angry mom 'relax, it's not a big deal.' You just added fuel to the fire!

idioms.meaning: Start over because the plan failed
idioms.memoryKey: Your business plan flopped. Time to erase the whiteboard and start fresh. Back to the drawing board = the current approach failed, let's redesign from scratch.

idioms.phrase: Bite off more than you can chew
idioms.meaning: To take on more responsibility than you can handle
idioms.memoryKey: Stuffing an entire samosa in your mouth — your cheeks explode, you can't chew. Like joining 5 coaching classes at once. You've bitten off more than you can chew!

idioms.phrase: Don't count your chickens before they hatch
idioms.meaning: Don't assume success before it happens
idioms.memoryKey: You have 10 eggs and already telling everyone you'll have 10 chickens. But what if 5 eggs don't hatch? Like celebrating IIM admission before the final merit list. Don't count your chickens!

idioms.phrase: Every cloud has a silver lining
idioms.meaning: There is something positive in every bad situation
idioms.memoryKey: Dark rain clouds look gloomy but the sun shining behind them creates a silver edge. Even your worst CAT mock teaches you something. Every bad situation has a hidden positive.

idioms.meaning: To become out of control
idioms.memoryKey: A kite string slips out of your hand and the kite flies away wildly. Like a small argument that escalates into a full fight — things got out of hand.

idioms.phrase: Give the benefit of the doubt
idioms.meaning: To trust someone despite uncertainty
idioms.memoryKey: Your friend says 'I didn't copy in the exam.' You're not sure, but you trust them anyway. Giving the benefit of the doubt = choosing to believe someone when you're unsure.

idioms.meaning: To study hard
idioms.memoryKey: Literally hitting your books — THWACK! Not with anger, but with dedication. When your parents say 'padhai kar', they mean hit the books = study seriously!

idioms.meaning: Something extra that makes a good situation even better
idioms.memoryKey: The cake is already delicious. But add icing? NOW it's perfect! Like getting a scholarship on top of your IIM admission — that's the icing on the cake!

idioms.phrase: Jack of all trades, master of none
idioms.meaning: Someone who can do many things but isn't expert at any
idioms.memoryKey: Jack tries coding, singing, cooking, painting — decent at all, expert at none. Like that student who joins every club but tops none. Jack of all trades, master of none.

idioms.meaning: Something very easy to do
idioms.memoryKey: Eating cake = easiest thing ever. Nobody struggles to eat cake! When someone says the exam was a piece of cake, it means it was ridiculously easy.

idioms.meaning: To remain neutral and not take sides
idioms.memoryKey: Two neighbours are fighting. You sit on the fence between their houses — literally in the middle, not choosing either side. Sit on the fence = refuse to pick a side in an argument.

quant_formulas.question: What is a Rational Number?
quant_formulas.answer:
A number expressible as $$\frac{p}{q}$$ where $$q \neq 0$$.

It has a terminating or recurring decimal.

quant_formulas.question: What is an Irrational Number?
quant_formulas.answer:
A number with a non-terminating, non-recurring decimal.

E.g., $$\sqrt{2}, \pi, e$$

quant_formulas.question: Every prime > 3 can be written as?
Is 1 prime or composite?
quant_formulas.answer:
1 is neither prime nor composite.

15 primes between 1–50.
10 primes between 50–100.

quant_formulas.question: Product of any number of odd numbers is?
quant_formulas.answer:
Always odd.

Product of numbers when at least one is even → always even.

quant_formulas.question: What is a Perfect Number?
quant_formulas.answer:
Sum of all factors excluding itself (including 1) = the number.

E.g., $$6 = 1+2+3$$

quant_formulas.question: BODMAS hierarchy?
quant_formulas.answer: Brackets → Of → Division, Multiplication → Addition, Subtraction

quant_formulas.question: Divisibility by $$2^n$$ or $$5^n$$?
quant_formulas.answer: A number formed by the last $$n$$ digits (left to right) must be divisible by $$2^n$$ or $$5^n$$.

quant_formulas.question: Divisibility rule for 6?
quant_formulas.answer: Must be divisible by both 2 AND 3.

quant_formulas.question: Divisibility rule for 7?
quant_formulas.answer:
Difference between number of tens and twice the units digit is divisible by 7.

E.g., $$4558 \to 455 - 16 = 439 \to 43 - 18 = 25$$ → Not divisible.

quant_formulas.question: Divisibility rule for 9?
quant_formulas.answer: Sum of all digits is a multiple of 9.

quant_formulas.question: Divisibility rule for 11?
quant_formulas.answer:
Sum of alternate digits is the same, or they differ by a multiple of 11.

E.g., $$7853245 \to |16-16| = 0$$ ✓

quant_formulas.question: Divisibility rule for 19?
quant_formulas.answer:
Sum of number of tens + twice the unit digit is divisible by 19.

E.g., $$4579 \to 457+18 = 475 \to 47+10 = 57$$ ✓

quant_formulas.question: If $$N = a^p \times b^q \times c^r$$, number of factors?
Ways to express $$N$$ as product of two factors?
If $$N$$ is a perfect square, ways to express as product of 2 different factors?
Sum of all factors of $$N = a^p \times b^q$$?
Ways of writing $$N$$ as product of 2 coprimes?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$2^{n-1}$$

where $$n$$ = number of different prime factors.

quant_formulas.question: Sum of coprimes to $$N$$ less than $$N$$?
Successive Division — how does it work?
quant_formulas.answer:
Divide step by step by successive divisors.

Product form: $$3 \times 4 \times 7 + 23$$
$$= ((1 \times 4+3) \times 3+2)$$

quant_formulas.question: $$a^n - b^n$$ is always divisible by?
quant_formulas.answer:
Always by $$(a-b)$$.

$$n$$ even → also by $$(a+b)$$.
$$n$$ odd → NOT by $$(a+b)$$.

quant_formulas.question: $$a^n + b^n$$ is divisible by?
quant_formulas.answer:
NEVER by $$(a-b)$$.

$$n$$ odd → by $$(a+b)$$.
$$n$$ even → NOT by $$(a+b)$$.

quant_formulas.question: Product of 2 consecutive numbers divisible by?
Out of $$n$$ consecutive integers?
quant_formulas.answer:
Exactly one is divisible by $$n$$.

Product of $$n$$ consecutive integers is divisible by $$n!$$

quant_formulas.question: Tens digit of a perfect square ending in 6?
If a perfect square ends with 5, tens digit?
Product of $$m$$-digit and $$n$$-digit number has how many digits?
$$n$$th root of $$M$$-digit number — how many digits?
quant_formulas.answer:
If $$\lceil M/n \rceil$$ is integer → that many digits.

Otherwise → $$\lfloor M/n \rfloor + 1$$ digits.

quant_formulas.question: Remainder shortcuts?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\frac{15}{7} \to$$ remainder 1.

Use successive division or digit-sum methods for larger numbers.

quant_formulas.question: $$\text{LCM} \times \text{HCF} = ?$$
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\text{LCM} \times \text{HCF} = \text{Product of the two numbers}$$

LCM is always a multiple of HCF.

quant_formulas.question: HCF and LCM of fractions?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\text{HCF} = \frac{\text{HCF of numerators}}{\text{LCM of denominators}}$$

$$\text{LCM} = \frac{\text{LCM of numerators}}{\text{HCF of denominators}}$$

quant_formulas.question: Number divided by $$p,q,r$$ leaves SAME remainder $$s$$?
Divided by $$p,q,r$$ with remainders where $$p-s = q-t = r-u = v$$?
Largest number dividing $$p,q,r$$ giving SAME remainder?
Last digit of powers follows?
quant_formulas.answer:
Cyclic pattern with period 4.

E.g., powers of 3: $$3,9,7,1,3,9,7,1\ldots$$

quant_formulas.question: Last 2 digits of powers when $$a$$ ends in 0?
quant_formulas.answer: All higher powers have at least 2 zeros at end.

quant_formulas.question: Last 2 digits of powers when $$a$$ ends in 5?
quant_formulas.answer: End in 25 or 75 depending on tens digit being even or odd.

quant_formulas.question: Last 2 digits when $$a$$ ends in 1,3,7,9?
quant_formulas.answer: Follow a cycle of 20 (or factors of 20).

quant_formulas.question: Last 2 digits when $$a$$ ends in 2,4,6,8?
quant_formulas.answer: $$4^{k}$$ cycle pattern. All subsequent series repeat.

quant_formulas.question: $$N$$ divided by $$D_n = \underbrace{99\ldots9}_{n}$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
Group digits of $$N$$ in sets of $$n$$ from right, add all groups → $$S_n$$.

Remainder $$= S_n \mod D_n$$

quant_formulas.question: $$x^n$$ ends in 76 whenever?
quant_formulas.answer: $$x$$ is even and $$n$$ is a multiple of 20.

quant_formulas.question: $$\frac{a}{b}$$ ratio types?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$a > b \to$$ ratio of greater inequality
$$a < b \to$$ ratio of lesser inequality
$$a = b \to$$ ratio of equality

quant_formulas.question: $$a:b :: c:d$$ — what are the terms called?
If $$a:b :: b:c$$ then?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$ac = b^2$$

$$c$$ is 3rd proportion of $$a$$ & $$b$$.
$$b$$ is mean proportion of $$a$$ & $$c$$.

quant_formulas.question: 2 articles sold at same price, one at $$p\%$$ profit, other at $$p\%$$ loss?
When can $$y$$ be uniquely determined from 2 equations with 3 unknowns?
Linearly independent equations for 3 unknowns?
quant_formulas.answer:
Need 3 equations.

$$I_3 \neq I_1 + kI_2$$ (no equation is a linear combination of others)

quant_formulas.question: Any point on perpendicular bisector is?
Parallel lines cut by transversal?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$l \parallel m \parallel n \implies \frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF}$$

Corresponding angles equal, alternate angles equal.

quant_formulas.question: Four centres of a triangle?
quant_formulas.answer:
Altitude → Orthocentre
Circumscribed circle → Circumcentre
Median → Centroid
Angle bisector → Incentre

quant_formulas.question: In isosceles triangle, altitude to base?
quant_formulas.answer:
Altitude = $$\perp$$ bisector = Angle bisector = Median

(All four coincide on the base)

quant_formulas.question: Triangle inequality?
quant_formulas.answer:
Sum of any 2 sides $$>$$ 3rd side.

Difference of any 2 sides $$<$$ 3rd side.

quant_formulas.question: If $$a < b < c$$ (sides), then angles?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\alpha < \beta < \gamma$$

Angles opposite to sides follow the same order.

quant_formulas.question: Acute vs Obtuse triangle — cosine rule?
quant_formulas.answer:
Acute: $$AC^2 = AB^2 + BC^2 - 2 \cdot BC \cdot BD$$

Obtuse: $$AC^2 = AB^2 + BC^2 + 2 \cdot BC \cdot BD$$

quant_formulas.question: Internal bisector divides opposite side in?
Circumcentre location?
quant_formulas.answer:
Acute → Inside
Right → Midpoint of hypotenuse
Obtuse → Outside

quant_formulas.question: Orthocentre location?
quant_formulas.answer:
Acute → Inside
Right → At the right-angle vertex
Obtuse → Outside

quant_formulas.question: Excentre — what is it?
quant_formulas.answer:
Internal bisector of one angle + external bisectors of other two meet at Excentre.

3 Excentres per triangle.

quant_formulas.question: Centroid divides median in ratio?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$2:1$$ from vertex to midpoint of opposite side.

$$\frac{AG}{GD} = \frac{2}{1}$$

quant_formulas.question: Equilateral triangle — which centres coincide?
quant_formulas.answer:
Centroid = Orthocentre = Circumcentre = Incentre

All four coincide!

quant_formulas.question: Equilateral triangle: circumradius to inradius?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\frac{R}{r} = \frac{2}{1}$$

Area $$= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}a^2$$

Each median creates 6 triangles of equal area.

quant_formulas.question: Two triangles are similar if?
quant_formulas.answer:
(i) All 3 angles respectively equal.
(ii) 2 sides proportional AND included angle equal.

quant_formulas.question: For similar triangles, ratio of sides = ratio of?
quant_formulas.answer:
Altitudes = Medians = Angle bisectors = Inradii = Circumradii = Perimeters

Also: $$AB^2 = BD \cdot BC$$

quant_formulas.question: Midpoint theorem?
quant_formulas.answer:
Line joining midpoints of 2 sides is $$\parallel$$ to 3rd side and = half its length.

$$AD \parallel BC$$ and Area $$\triangle ABC = $$ Area $$\triangle BDC$$

quant_formulas.question: Area of regular polygon?
quant_formulas.answer: $$\frac{1}{2} \times \text{Perimeter} \times \perp \text{ dist from centre to any side}$$

quant_formulas.question: Inradius of a triangle is always less than?
Right $$\triangle$$ with fixed hypotenuse — max area when?
Right $$\triangle$$ area with inradius $$r$$ and circumradius $$R$$?
Quadrilateral: offsets?
quant_formulas.answer: Perpendiculars drawn from opposite vertices to a diagonal.

quant_formulas.question: Cyclic quadrilateral: opposite angles?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$A + D = 180°$$

Exterior angle = Interior opposite angle.

quant_formulas.question: Parallelogram properties?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\alpha+\beta = 180°$$

Each diagonal → 2 congruent $$\triangle$$s.
Diagonals bisect each other.

Area: $$\triangle PAB + \triangle PCD = \frac{1}{2}$$ of $$\parallel$$gm

quant_formulas.question: $$\parallel$$gm and $$\triangle$$ with same base between same $$\parallel$$ lines?
Rhombus diagonals property?
quant_formulas.answer:
Bisect each other at $$90°$$.

4 congruent $$\triangle$$s formed.

quant_formulas.question: Largest rectangle inscribed in a circle?
Convex polygon: interior angle?
quant_formulas.answer:
Each interior angle $$< 180°$$.

Any straight line crosses only 2 sides.

quant_formulas.question: Regular polygon: sum of all interior angles?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$(n-2) \times 180°$$

All sides and angles equal. Can be inscribed in a circle.

quant_formulas.question: Regular polygon: centre joined to each vertex?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$n$$ identical isosceles $$\triangle$$s.

Hexagon → all equilateral.

Diagonals = $$\binom{n}{2} - n$$

quant_formulas.question: Tangents from external point?
quant_formulas.answer:
2 tangents, equal length.

Tangent $$\perp$$ radius at point of contact.

quant_formulas.question: $$\perp$$ from centre to chord?
quant_formulas.answer:
Bisects the chord.

2 chords equidistant from centre are equal.

quant_formulas.question: Angle in a semicircle?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$90°$$

Inscribed angle $$= \frac{1}{2} \times$$ central angle.

Angle subtended by diameter $$= 90°$$

quant_formulas.question: Angles in same segment?
quant_formulas.answer:
Equal.

$$\angle ABC + \angle ADC = 180°$$ (cyclic quad).

Alternate segment theorem: angle between tangent & chord = angle in alternate segment.

quant_formulas.question: Alternate segment theorem?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\angle STP = \angle SRT$$

Angle between tangent and chord = angle subtended by chord in alternate segment.

quant_formulas.question: Degree of remainder vs divisor?
quant_formulas.answer: Degree of remainder is always less than degree of divisor.

quant_formulas.question: $$f(x) = a_nx^n+\ldots+a_0$$: sum of roots taken $$m$$ at a time?
Max positive roots by Descartes' rule?
quant_formulas.answer:
= Number of sign changes $$k$$.

Positive roots: $$k, k-2, k-4, \ldots$$

Negative roots: from $$f(-x)=0$$

quant_formulas.question: Two quadratics with one common root — condition?
AM $$\geq$$ GM $$\geq$$ HM?
quant_formulas.answer:
Always true for positive numbers.

Equality only when all numbers are equal.

If $$a+b=$$ const, $$\max(ab)$$ when $$a=b$$.
If $$ab=$$ const, $$\min(a+b)$$ when $$a=b$$.

quant_formulas.question: AM-GM application for products?
quant_formulas.answer:
Min of $$(a+k)(b+k)$$ → use AM-GM.

Extends to 3+ quantities.

quant_formulas.question: HP: definition?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\frac{1}{a},\; \frac{1}{a+d},\; \frac{1}{a+2d},\ldots$$

Reciprocals are in AP. No direct sum formula.

quant_formulas.question: Finding $$t_n$$ from $$S_n$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$t_n = S_n - S_{n-1}$$

Use standard sum formulas.

quant_formulas.question: Characteristic and Mantissa?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\log_a N = \text{abc.xyz}$$

abc → characteristic (integer part)
xyz → mantissa (decimal part)

For $$N>1$$: characteristic = (digits - 1)

quant_formulas.question: Characteristic when $$N < 1$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
Magnitude = 1 + (number of zeros after decimal point).

It is negative: e.g., $$\log 0.02 = \bar{2} + 0.301$$

quant_formulas.question: Mantissa property?
quant_formulas.answer:
Same for all numbers with same significant digits.

$$\log 2 = 0.301$$

quant_formulas.question: Log product and quotient rules?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\log(mn) = \log m + \log n$$

$$\log\frac{m}{n} = \log m - \log n$$

$$\log m^p = p \cdot \log m$$

quant_formulas.question: Arrangements with repetition: $$p$$ alike, $$q$$ alike, $$r$$ alike?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\frac{n!}{p!\; q!\; r!}$$

Only when ALL $$n$$ are selected.

quant_formulas.question: Each item used any number of times, $$r$$ positions?
Dividing $$(p+q)$$ items into groups of $$p$$ and $$q$$?
Sum of all $$n$$-digit numbers from $$n$$ distinct digits?
Squares and Rectangles in $$n \times n$$ grid?
Independent events?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B)$$

Mutual independence (3 events): each pair independent AND
$$P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)$$

quant_formulas.question: Card deck basics?
quant_formulas.answer:
4 suits: Clubs, Hearts, Diamonds, Spades.
Honours: Ace, King, Queen, Jack.
Total = 52.

quant_formulas.question: Leap year rule?
quant_formulas.answer:
Divisible by 4 ✓ (2004, 2008)
Divisible by 100 but not 400 ✗ (2100, 2200)
Divisible by 400 ✓ (2400)

Odds in favour $$= \frac{\text{favourable}}{\text{unfavourable}}$$

quant_formulas.question: Difference between CI and SI for 2 years?
Replacement: $$P$$ volume liquid, $$Q$$ removed and replaced $$n$$ times?
Average Speed?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}}$$

NOT the average of speeds.

quant_formulas.question: Circular track, same direction — first meet?
Time to meet at starting point?
Two people P→Q, after meeting?
quant_formulas.answer:
1st meeting: combined distance $$= d$$.
2nd onwards: $$= 2d$$ each.

If after 1st meet, $$a$$ took $$t_1$$ and $$b$$ took $$t_2$$:
$$t_{\text{meet}} = \sqrt{t_1 \cdot t_2}$$

quant_formulas.question: $$PQ = 3a - b$$ context?
quant_formulas.answer: When P starts first, head-start distance and meeting relationships.

quant_formulas.question: In 12 hours, hands at $$0°$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
11 times. Every $$\frac{12}{11}$$ hrs $$= 1$$ hr $$5\frac{5}{11}$$ min.

$$180°$$: 11 times.
$$90°$$ or any other angle: 22 times.

quant_formulas.question: Prism?
quant_formulas.answer:
LSA $$= \text{Perimeter}_{\text{base}} \times h$$

TSA $$=$$ LSA $$+ 2 \times$$ Area$$_{\text{base}}$$

Volume $$=$$ Area$$_{\text{base}} \times h$$

quant_formulas.question: Pyramid?
quant_formulas.answer:
Volume $$= \frac{1}{3} \times$$ Area$$_{\text{base}} \times h$$

LSA $$= \frac{1}{2} \times$$ Perimeter$$_{\text{base}} \times l$$

quant_formulas.question: Cone?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$l^2 = r^2+h^2$$

Volume $$= \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$$

CSA $$= \pi r l$$

TSA $$= \pi r(l+r)$$

quant_formulas.question: Torus?
quant_formulas.answer:
SA $$= 4\pi^2 ra$$

Volume $$= 2\pi^2 r^2 a$$

$$r$$ = tube radius, $$a$$ = centre-to-tube distance.

quant_formulas.question: Circle vs inscribed/circumscribed polygon?
quant_formulas.answer:
Circumference $$>$$ perimeter of any inscribed polygon.

Perimeter of circumscribing polygon $$>$$ circumference.

quant_formulas.question: Translation of axes?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$x = X+h,\quad y = Y+k$$

Origin shifted to $$(h,k)$$.

quant_formulas.question: $$\sin(A+B)$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B$$

$$\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$$

quant_formulas.question: $$\sin(A-B)$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B$$

$$\cos(A-B) = \cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B$$

quant_formulas.question: $$\tan(A+B)$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$\frac{\tan A+\tan B}{1-\tan A\tan B}$$

$$\tan(A-B) = \frac{\tan A-\tan B}{1+\tan A\tan B}$$

quant_formulas.question: Product-to-sum formulas?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$2\sin A\cos B = \sin(A+B)+\sin(A-B)$$
$$2\cos A\cos B = \cos(A+B)+\cos(A-B)$$
$$2\sin A\sin B = \cos(A-B)-\cos(A+B)$$

quant_formulas.question: When $$A+B+C=180°$$?
quant_formulas.answer: $$\tan A+\tan B+\tan C = \tan A \cdot \tan B \cdot \tan C$$

quant_formulas.question: Cardinality?
quant_formulas.answer: Number of distinct elements. Order doesn't matter.

quant_formulas.question: Subsets of set with cardinality $$n$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$2^n$$

Power set = set of all subsets.
$$\phi$$ is a subset of every set.

quant_formulas.question: $$A-B$$ and Complement?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$A-B=$$ elements in $$A$$ not in $$B$$.

$$A' = A^c = U-A$$

quant_formulas.question: De Morgan's Laws?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$$

$$(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$$

quant_formulas.question: Cartesian product $$A \times B$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$O(A)=m,\; O(B)=n \implies O(A \times B) = mn$$

$$A \times B \neq B \times A$$ but $$n(A \times B)=n(B \times A)$$

quant_formulas.question: Function definition?
quant_formulas.answer:
Each element of $$A$$ maps to exactly one element of $$B$$.

Range $$\subseteq$$ Co-domain.

quant_formulas.question: Bijection?
quant_formulas.answer:
One-one AND onto.
$$n(A)=n(B)$$

Number of bijections $$= n!$$

quant_formulas.question: Base systems?
quant_formulas.answer:
Binary: base 2 $$(0,1)$$
Octal: base 8
Decimal: base 10
Hexadecimal: base 16

quant_formulas.question: Decimal to other base?
quant_formulas.answer:
Repeated division by base.
Read remainders bottom-to-top.

$$(23)_{10} = (10111)_2$$

quant_formulas.question: Decimal fraction to binary?
quant_formulas.answer:
Multiply fraction by 2 repeatedly, note integer parts.

$$(0.375)_{10} = (0.011)_2$$

quant_formulas.question: Median?
quant_formulas.answer:
Middle value when sorted.

For $$n$$ values:
Odd: $$\frac{n+1}{2}$$th term.
Even: average of $$\frac{n}{2}$$th and $$\frac{n}{2}+1$$th terms.

quant_formulas.question: Mode?
quant_formulas.answer: Value occurring the highest number of times.

quant_formulas.question: Mean Deviation?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$M.D. = \frac{\sum|x_i-A|}{n}$$

Least when taken about the Median.

quant_formulas.question: SD transformation: if S.D. = 6, find S.D. of $$(2x_i+3)$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$= 2 \times 6 = 12$$

Adding constant → no change.
Multiplying by $$k$$ → S.D. $$\times |k|$$

quant_formulas.question: Distribution table: ALL, NO, Some, Some not?
quant_formulas.answer:
ALL: Subject ✓, Predicate ✗
NO: Both ✓
Some: Both ✗
Some not: Subject ✗, Predicate ✓

quant_formulas.question: Middle term rule?
quant_formulas.answer: Must be distributed at least once.

quant_formulas.question: Negation: If $$p$$, then $$q$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
Negation: $$p \wedge \neg q$$

Unless $$p$$, then $$q$$: $$\neg p \wedge \neg q$$

Only if $$p$$, then $$q$$: $$\neg p \wedge q$$

quant_formulas.question: Contrapositive of $$p \to q$$?
quant_formulas.answer:
$$
eg q 	o 
eg p$$

Converse: $$q 	o p$$
Inverse: $$
eg p 	o 
eg q$$

lrdi_tips.question: First 2 minutes of any LRDI set — what should you do?
lrdi_tips.answer: READ the entire set without solving. Understand the structure: how many entities, how many parameters, what constraints exist. Jumping in without reading = guaranteed mistakes.

lrdi_tips.question: How to pick which LRDI set to attempt first?
lrdi_tips.answer: Scan ALL sets in 3–4 minutes. Pick the one with maximum definite information and minimum ambiguity. Arrangement > Scheduling > Venn Diagram > Games & Tournaments (usually).

lrdi_tips.question: Linear arrangement — where to start?
lrdi_tips.answer: Start with the person who has the MOST constraints. Place them first, then build around them. Never start with the least constrained person.

lrdi_tips.question: Circular arrangement — key difference from linear?
lrdi_tips.answer: No fixed starting point. Fix one person and arrange rest relative to them. If N people, arrangements = (N-1)! Also: left/right flips in circular = clockwise/anticlockwise.

lrdi_tips.question: How to handle "not adjacent" conditions?
lrdi_tips.answer:
Method 1: Total arrangements − adjacent arrangements.
Method 2: Place others first, then fill gaps.

Always draw it out — don't try to do it mentally.

lrdi_tips.question: Table with missing values — first step?
lrdi_tips.answer: Fill in ALL values that can be directly calculated from given data. Then look for rows/columns with only ONE unknown — solve those next. Cascade: each solved cell may unlock another.

lrdi_tips.question: When a table has "at least" or "at most" conditions?
lrdi_tips.answer: Use boundary values. "At least 3" means minimum is 3. "At most 5" means maximum is 5. Mark ranges for each cell, then use row/column totals to narrow down.

lrdi_tips.question: 3-set Venn Diagram — formula?
lrdi_tips.answer:
n(A∪B∪C) = n(A)+n(B)+n(C) − n(A∩B) − n(B∩C) − n(A∩C) + n(A∩B∩C).

Always fill from the INSIDE OUT — start with the center (all three), then pairs, then singles.

lrdi_tips.question: "Only A" vs "A" — what's the difference?
lrdi_tips.answer: "A" includes everyone in A (even those in A∩B, A∩C, A∩B∩C). "Only A" excludes all overlaps. CAT loves testing this distinction. Always clarify which one the question asks.

lrdi_tips.question: Round-robin tournament: total matches?
lrdi_tips.answer:
Each pair plays once: ⁿC₂ = n(n-1)/2 matches.

Total points distributed = total matches (if win=1, loss=0) or 2× matches (if win=2, draw=1, loss=0).

lrdi_tips.question: Knockout tournament: total matches to find winner?
lrdi_tips.answer: Always n-1 matches (because each match eliminates exactly 1 player). True regardless of seeding or byes.

lrdi_tips.question: How to approach tournament problems with partial results?
lrdi_tips.answer: Draw the complete bracket/table structure first. Fill known results. Use total points constraints (row totals = points earned by each team) to deduce unknowns.

lrdi_tips.question: Scheduling problems — key constraint to identify first?
lrdi_tips.answer: Find the bottleneck: which resource/person/room has the tightest schedule? Build around that. Also check: are there any mutual exclusions (A and B can't happen at same time)?

lrdi_tips.question: Fastest way to solve blood relations?
lrdi_tips.answer: Draw a family tree as you read. Males on one side, females on other. Use symbols: = for married, | for parent-child, -- for siblings. Never try to solve without a diagram.

lrdi_tips.question: Coded blood relations — how to decode?
lrdi_tips.answer: Decode the relationship words first (e.g., A#B means A is father of B). Write out the actual relationships. THEN draw the tree. Two-step process — don't mix decoding with tree-building.

lrdi_tips.question: Direction sense — common trap?
lrdi_tips.answer: LEFT and RIGHT are relative to the person's facing direction, not yours! Always track which direction the person is facing after each turn. Draw arrows.

lrdi_tips.question: Painted cube cut into smaller cubes — corner/edge/face pieces?
lrdi_tips.answer:
If cut into n³ smaller cubes:
3 faces painted (corners): always 8
2 faces painted (edges): 12(n-2)
1 face painted (faces): 6(n-2)²
0 faces painted (inside): (n-2)³

lrdi_tips.question: Opposite faces of a standard dice always sum to?
lrdi_tips.answer: 7. Pairs: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4). If you can see 3 faces, you can determine all 6.

lrdi_tips.question: Liar-Truth teller problems — how to approach?
lrdi_tips.answer: Assume one person is telling the truth. Check if all statements are consistent. If contradiction → that person is lying. Try the other assumption. Only one scenario will be consistent.

lrdi_tips.question: How to handle "exactly one of us is lying" type statements?
lrdi_tips.answer: These are self-referential. If A says "exactly one of us is lying" — test both cases (A lies vs A tells truth). The trick: a liar saying "exactly one lies" means the reality is different from "exactly one."

lrdi_tips.question: What if you're stuck on a set for 5+ minutes?
lrdi_tips.answer: MOVE ON. Mark it and come back if time permits. Spending 15 minutes on one set kills your score. Better to attempt 4 sets at 80% accuracy than 2 sets at 100%.

lrdi_tips.question: How to handle "cannot be determined" options in LRDI?
lrdi_tips.answer: This is ONLY correct when there are genuinely multiple valid configurations. If you can find even ONE additional constraint that pins down the answer, it CAN be determined. Check your work.

lrdi_tips.question: Data sufficiency — common mistake?
lrdi_tips.answer: Don't solve! Just check if the data IS SUFFICIENT to solve. Students waste time computing exact answers. The question is about possibility, not calculation.

lrdi_tips.question: Order of solving questions within a set?
lrdi_tips.answer: Answer definite questions first (those with unique answers). Skip "possible" questions initially. Come back to possibility-based questions after you've maximized constraints from the definite ones.

lrdi_tips.question: If P then Q — what can we conclude from Q being true?
lrdi_tips.answer: NOTHING about P. Common trap! "If it rains, ground is wet" does NOT mean "ground is wet implies it rained" (could be a sprinkler). Only contrapositive is valid: not Q → not P.

lrdi_tips.question: Rectangular table with people on both sides — key trap?
lrdi_tips.answer: People sitting OPPOSITE each other face each other. People sitting on the SAME side face the same direction. Don't confuse 'left of' on the same side vs opposite side — left flips when facing directions change.

lrdi_tips.question: How to handle 'A sits 3 places to the right of B'?
lrdi_tips.answer: Draw the arrangement. Count seats from B going rightward (clockwise in circular). 3 places means 3 seats away, NOT 3 people between them. Common CAT trap: '3 places to the right' ≠ '3 people between them'.

lrdi_tips.question: What if circular arrangement has conditions like 'A and B are not adjacent'?
lrdi_tips.answer: Fix one person (say A). Place everyone else. For 'not adjacent' — place B in any seat EXCEPT the two seats next to A. Total valid spots = (n-3) for B out of (n-1) remaining seats.

lrdi_tips.question: Multiple events, multiple rooms, time slots — first step?
lrdi_tips.answer: Draw a grid: rows = time slots, columns = rooms. Fill in fixed/known events first. Then apply constraints one by one. Key: look for time slots or rooms with only 1 option left.

lrdi_tips.question: How to handle 'Event A must happen before Event B' constraints?
lrdi_tips.answer: Create a precedence chain: A → B → C. Draw arrows showing which must come first. Then fit into available slots. Multiple precedence chains? Find where they overlap or conflict.

lrdi_tips.question: Distributing items to people with 'at least' and 'at most' constraints?
lrdi_tips.answer: Start with the TIGHTEST constraint. If A gets at least 4 and at most 6 out of 20 total, try A=4 first and see what remains. Then A=5, A=6. Enumerate systematically — don't guess.

lrdi_tips.question: 'Each person gets a different number of items' — shortcut?
lrdi_tips.answer: If n people get distinct counts from a total T, the minimum total = 0+1+2+...+(n-1) = n(n-1)/2. If T is much larger, the maximum goes to the person with fewest constraints. Start from min allocation and distribute surplus.

lrdi_tips.question: Ranking problems: 'A scored more than B but less than C' — how to track?
lrdi_tips.answer: Write inequalities: C > A > B. Chain them. When you have 5-6 people, maintain a single sorted list. Insert each new person into the right position. If the final order has gaps (unknown relative positions), note them.

lrdi_tips.question: 'Exactly 3 people scored between A and B' — what does this mean?
lrdi_tips.answer: It means in the ranking, there are exactly 3 people whose scores are strictly between A's and B's scores. A could be above or below B. Consider both cases unless specified.

lrdi_tips.question: 'A committee of 5 from 10 people with constraints' — LRDI approach?
lrdi_tips.answer: This isn't P&C — it's logic! List constraints: 'If A is selected, B must be selected', 'C and D cannot both be selected'. Start with the most restrictive constraint. Build valid committees one by one.

lrdi_tips.question: How to handle 'If A then B' and 'If B then not C' together?
lrdi_tips.answer: Chain them: A → B → not C. So if A is selected, C is automatically OUT. Contrapositive: C → not B → not A. Build an implication map before trying combinations.

lrdi_tips.question: Shortest path problems in LRDI?
lrdi_tips.answer: Draw the network. Label each edge with distance/time. Use systematic exploration: start from source, track cumulative distance at each node. Don't just eyeball — one missed shorter path ruins everything.

lrdi_tips.question: 'How many distinct routes from A to B without repeating any node?'
lrdi_tips.answer: Enumerate paths systematically. Tree method: branch at each junction, prune branches that revisit a node. Count only complete paths reaching B. For small networks (5-7 nodes), this is faster than any formula.

lrdi_tips.question: Maxima-minima in Venn diagrams?
lrdi_tips.answer: To MAXIMIZE an intersection, push as many elements as possible into the overlap. To MINIMIZE, spread elements apart. Use: max(A∩B) = min(A,B). min(A∩B) = max(0, A+B-Total).

lrdi_tips.question: 4-set Venn diagram — is it possible?
lrdi_tips.answer: Yes, but NOT with circles — you need ellipses or rectangles. In CAT, 4-set problems usually use a table/matrix instead of a diagram. Convert to a grid with 2 parameters on rows and 2 on columns.

lrdi_tips.question: Table where row totals and column totals are given but individual cells unknown?
lrdi_tips.answer: Start with rows/columns that have the fewest unknowns. If a row has 4 cells and 3 are known, the 4th = row total minus the 3 knowns. Each solved cell cascades into solving cells in its column.

lrdi_tips.question: What if the table has percentages instead of absolute numbers?
lrdi_tips.answer: Convert everything to the same unit first. If you know any one absolute value, you can derive all others from percentages. If no absolute value is given, work entirely in ratios — actual values may not be needed.

lrdi_tips.question: League format with ties in points table — how to resolve?
lrdi_tips.answer: Check if additional info is given (head-to-head, goal difference, etc.). If not, list all valid configurations. CAT often asks 'which is definitely true?' — only pick what holds in ALL valid scenarios.

lrdi_tips.question: Tournament with group stage + knockouts — what to track?
lrdi_tips.answer: Two separate structures. Group stage: use a points table (wins, losses, draws, points). Knockout: draw a bracket. Bridge them: top 2 from each group enter knockouts. Don't mix up the formats.

lrdi_tips.question: Cube painted on all faces, cut into 64 smaller cubes. How many have exactly 2 painted faces?
lrdi_tips.answer: 64 = 4³, so n=4. Two painted faces = edge cubes (not corners). Formula: 12(n-2) = 12×2 = 24.

lrdi_tips.question: Two dice rolled — probability of same number?
lrdi_tips.answer: Total outcomes = 36. Same number: (1,1),(2,2)...(6,6) = 6. P = 6/36 = 1/6. In LRDI, this appears in game-based sets with dice mechanics.

lrdi_tips.question: 'At least one is a liar and at least one tells truth' — how to use?
lrdi_tips.answer: Eliminates 2 scenarios: all truth-tellers AND all liars. Test remaining combos: (1T,2L), (2T,1L). Only 6 combinations for 3 people. Usually only 1 is consistent with all statements.

lrdi_tips.question: A says B lies, B says C lies, C says A lies — who lies?
lrdi_tips.answer: If A=truth → B=liar → C=truth → A=liar. CONTRADICTION. So A=liar → B=truth → C=liar → A=truth. CONTRADICTION again. Means exactly 1 or 2 liars — test each systematically.

lrdi_tips.question: How to practice LRDI effectively?
lrdi_tips.answer: 1. Solve sets TIMED (15-20 min max). 2. If stuck after 10 min, look at the approach, not the answer. 3. Categorize weak areas. 4. Do 2 full sets daily. 5. Review wrong answers — find WHERE your logic broke.

lrdi_tips.question: CAT LRDI section — ideal time allocation?
lrdi_tips.answer: Total: 40 minutes for ~4-5 sets. Scan all sets: 3-4 min. Best set: 8-10 min. Second: 10-12 min. Third: 10-12 min. Fourth (partial): remaining time. NEVER spend >15 min on one set.

lrdi_tips.question: 'P only if Q' means?
lrdi_tips.answer: P → Q (same as 'if P then Q'). NOT 'if Q then P'. Trap: 'only if' reverses intuition. Think: P can happen ONLY IF Q is true, meaning P requires Q.

lrdi_tips.question: 'P if and only if Q' (biconditional)?
lrdi_tips.answer: P → Q AND Q → P. Both true together or false together. If P is true, Q must be true. If P is false, Q must be false. They're logically equivalent.

lrdi_tips.question: Negation of 'All cats are black'?
lrdi_tips.answer: 'Some cats are not black.' NOT 'no cats are black'. Negation of ALL = SOME...NOT. Negation of SOME = NONE.

lrdi_tips.question: Negation of 'If it rains, the ground is wet'?
lrdi_tips.answer: 'It rains AND the ground is NOT wet.' Negation of (P → Q) = P ∧ ¬Q. An implication is false only when premise is true but conclusion is false.

lrdi_tips.question: 'Definitely true' vs 'possibly true' — what's the difference?
lrdi_tips.answer: 'Definitely true' = true in ALL valid scenarios. Even one exception = not definite. 'Possibly true' = true in AT LEAST ONE scenario. CAT loves mixing these in options. Always check all configurations.
